one_line_takeaway: FSW anchors LLM ownership watermarks in a GEVP-derived subspace that balances task-criticality (Fisher information) against compression-induced variance, achieving near-perfect bit accuracy and low false-positive detection across five 7-8B backbones under fine-tuning/quantization/pruning/distillation attacks, though its own tables show markedly weaker detection-score retention and a large utility cost specifically on DeepSeek-7B-Chat, and its guarantees explicitly exclude fully unconstrained (from-scratch) distillation.
key_finding: On the five backbones (Table 1), FSW achieves 100% bit accuracy in the clean (no-attack) setting on all models, AUC ranging 0.894 (LLaMA-2-7B) to 1.000 (DeepSeek-7B-Chat), with utility cost (PPL increase) ranging from +0.37 (LLaMA-3-8B) to +17.79 (DeepSeek-7B-Chat, a notably large PPL jump from 59.52 to 77.31) and modest drops in HellaSwag/ARC-E accuracy (e.g. LLaMA-3-8B: HellaSwag 68.6%->67.8%, ARC-E 79.6%->75.8%). Under attacks (Table 2), detection scores and bit accuracy are generally retained but degrade unevenly: DeepSeek-7B-Chat is the weakest case, with bit accuracy stable at 100% but detection-score retention collapsing to 44.17% under backbone distillation and 17.60% under LoRA FT, and PPL increasing sharply post-attack (e.g. 77.31->92.76 under quantization, +15.45). Against baselines under distillation (Table 5), FSW ('Full DIFSW' in that table -- note this label differs from 'FSW' used elsewhere, an internal inconsistency in the paper) preserves much lower post-attack PPL for LLaMA-2-7B (5.91, unchanged) versus EmMark's 5.73 and Weight Quant.'s 5.78 (comparable), but for DeepSeek-7B, FSW's post-attack PPL (61.39) is markedly worse than EmMark's (7.95) or Weight Quant.'s (8.23), while FSW's pre-attack PPL is also already the worst of the group (77.31 vs EmMark 152.36 pre-attack... note EmMark pre-attack PPL is even higher, 152.36, so relative comparisons are mixed and paper does not explicitly discuss this DeepSeek regression). The ablation (Table 4) shows removing Adaptive Thresholding causes the largest degradation: post-attack bit accuracy falls from 87.5% (Full FSW) to 12.5%, and detection score S from -0.25 to -2.73 (delta). Bit-capacity ablation (Table 3, ECC): raw (No-ECC) bit accuracy is already 100% at 4, 12, 16, 32, 64 bits but only 87.5% at 8 bits; applying Hamming(7,4) fixes the 8-bit case to 100% message accuracy but paradoxically drops the 4-bit case to 0% (too few bits for the code to operate), which the paper does not explain. Training overhead (Qwen2.5-7B, Table 10): total FSW pipeline takes 1855.68s over 8001 steps vs. 1101.55s/7500 steps for standard fine-tuning, with peak GPU memory 56410.34MB vs. 29820.63MB baseline, dominated by the watermark-embedding phase (1764.38s).
core_contribution: The paper proposes Functional Subspace Watermarking (FSW), a white-box model-watermarking method for LLM ownership protection. Instead of embedding watermark signals into arbitrary weight or activation directions, FSW identifies a low-dimensional 'functional backbone' subspace by solving a Generalized Eigenvalue Problem (GEVP): Fu_i = lambda_i C_A u_i, where F is a Fisher information matrix (estimated over a calibration set) capturing task-critical sensitivity of representation directions, and C_A is a 'compression invariance' matrix capturing the expected variance a direction incurs under a family of simulated compression operators (linear projection, Gaussian quantization noise, structural/Bernoulli dropout). Directions are ranked by the generalized eigenvalue (interpreted as a robustness score), and an adaptive spectral truncation step selects a middle band of directions (thresholds tau_lower, tau_upper relative to the top eigenvalue lambda_1) rather than the top-k or bottom-k directions, on the stated grounds that top eigenvalue directions are too task-critical (embedding there destroys utility) and bottom directions are too unstable (easily removed). The selected directions form a projection basis U*. Ownership bits are encoded as signs against M mutually orthogonal secret key vectors in this subspace via a margin-based hinge loss (Eq. 8), jointly trained with the LM loss and a 'vector consistency' loss that pulls the projected representation toward that of the frozen original model (to preserve semantic behavior). Verification computes an aggregated statistic S over a challenge set; under the null (unwatermarked) hypothesis S is modeled as Gaussian, giving a closed-form FPR bound via the complementary error function. Multi-bit messages are recovered via per-key-vector sign decoding plus Hamming(7,4) error-correcting codes.
threat_model: The paper's own Section 3 threat model: a model owner releases a watermarked LLM; an adversary with white-box access to model parameters seeks to remove or invalidate the embedded signature while preserving the model's downstream utility. Permitted adversary actions (drawn from the paper's own experiments, Table 2 and Table 5): supervised/LoRA fine-tuning, weight pruning, INT4-style quantization, additive Gaussian noise, and knowledge/backbone distillation. The paper explicitly restricts its guarantees with a 'Functional Preservation Constraint': attacks are assumed to preserve the model's internal functional backbone representations, and the authors state their guarantees do NOT extend to 'fully unconstrained distillation where the student model is free to arbitrarily reparameterize its latent space' -- i.e., the threat model excludes adversaries who train a student from scratch with no architectural/representational continuity to the teacher. The defender is assumed to have white-box access to the suspect model for verification (inspecting parameters/activations), not just black-box query access.
limitations: The paper explicitly states its own robustness guarantee does not cover 'fully unconstrained distillation where the student model is free to arbitrarily reparameterize its latent space' -- i.e., it assumes an adversary that preserves internal functional structure, not an adversary training a genuinely independent student architecture from scratch. Numerically, the DeepSeek-7B-Chat results in Tables 1, 2, and 5 show the method's weakest case: a large clean-setting PPL cost (+17.79) and the worst retention of detection score under both LoRA fine-tuning (17.60%) and backbone distillation (44.17%) among all five backbones, though bit accuracy for DeepSeek stays at 100% even as the continuous detection-score margin erodes -- the paper reports this without offering an explanation for why DeepSeek is the outlier. The Mistral-7B statistical test (Table 7) fails at the most stringent significance level tested (alpha=1e-8, margin -0.2883, marked with an X in the paper's own table), meaning ownership could not be established with the same confidence as for DeepSeek-7B at that threshold, though it succeeds at alpha=1e-6. The component ablation and bit-capacity ablation are conducted only on LLaMA-2-7B-hf, so it is unclear whether the same failure modes (e.g., the 4-bit ECC anomaly, or the specific sensitivity to Adaptive Thresholding removal) generalize across the other four backbones. Baseline comparisons (Table 5) are reported only under the distillation attack, not under quantization/pruning/LoRA, so the paper does not substantiate a full SOTA comparison across all attack types claimed in the abstract. Training-cost accounting (Table 10) is reported for only one model (Qwen2.5-7B), leaving open how overhead scales with model size. The paper's own tables use inconsistent method naming ('Full DIFSW' in Table 5 versus 'FSW' elsewhere), a presentation inconsistency worth noting though not necessarily a substantive flaw.

datasets_used: WikiText-2 (500 samples for Fisher/invariance matrix estimation, 500 separate samples for perplexity evaluation)
C4 / Multimodal C4 (3,000 sequences for watermark-embedding LoRA fine-tuning)
quantitative_results: Clean-setting bit accuracy: 100% on all five backbones (LLaMA-2-7B, LLaMA-3-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, DeepSeek-7B-Chat), Table 1
Detection-score retention under attacks (Table 2): LLaMA-2-7B backbone distillation 95.90%, LoRA FT 111.28% (increase), quantization 107.69%; DeepSeek-7B backbone distillation 44.17%, LoRA FT 17.60%

Statistical significance margins (Table 7, appendix): DeepSeek-7B margin S-T_alpha = 3.0587 at alpha=1e-8 (detected); Mistral-7B margin = -0.2883 at alpha=1e-8 (not detected), 0.3776 at alpha=1e-6 (detected)
Ablation (Table 4, LLaMA-2-7B): removing Adaptive Thresholding drops post-attack bit accuracy from 87.5% (Full FSW) to 12.5% and detection score S to -2.73
ECC ablation (Table 3, LLaMA-2-7B): Hamming(7,4) raises 8-bit message accuracy from 87.5% (no ECC) to 100%, but 4-bit message accuracy drops from 100% (no ECC) to 0% with Hamming(7,4)
Training overhead (Table 10, Qwen2.5-7B): FSW total 1855.68s/8001 steps and 56410.34 MB peak GPU memory vs. standard FT 1101.55s/7500 steps and 29820.63 MB
baselines_compared: Clean FT (unwatermarked fine-tuning, utility upper bound)
Naive Top-k (internal ablation variant: GEVP subspace without adaptive spectral truncation, i.e. selecting top-eigenvalue directions directly)
